es.meaning: Good luck — wished directly, no ritual attached.

it.meaning: Good luck — with a ritual reply: never grazie, always 'crepi (il lupo)'.
it.exampleTranslation: “My exam's tomorrow.” “Good luck!” “Thanks!” (lit. may it die!)

bridge.note: An asymmetry, and the asymmetry is the lesson: Spanish wishes luck head-on — mucha suerte, answered with gracias, done. Italian superstitiously refuses to name luck at all: buona fortuna is felt to invite the opposite, so you are sent into the wolf's mouth instead — and the ritual has a scripted reply. Never grazie; always Crepi! (may the wolf die). One language has a ceremony where the other has a sentence.

interference.es_to_it: Two stumbles: calquing suerte gives 'buona fortuna', which superstitious Italians sidestep — and answering grazie to in bocca al lupo breaks the ritual. The reply is crepi.
interference.it_to_es: Nothing to import: there is no wolf, no jinx, no scripted reply. Mucha suerte, and gracias is the correct answer.

review.notes: Discovered via FluentU list (Joe, 2026-07-16). FIRST PAIRING REJECTED by editor same day: '¡Mucha mierda!' (theatre, vulgar) was judged both crude and a mismatch for Italy's universal wish — correct on both counts. Re-paired with the true everyday counterpart; the ritual/no-ritual asymmetry is now the payload. IT reviewer to confirm crepi etiquette framing.

cue: How do you wish someone luck before an exam?
